Of the 271 bugs found, 180 were classified as sec-high (exploitable through normal user behavior like web browsing), 80 as sec-moderate, and 11 as sec-low. Mozilla has published full Bugzilla reports for 12 of these vulnerabilities with reproducible test cases, all meeting the company's standard criteria for security classification.

## Clearing the Hype-to-Reality Gap

The findings matter because they address a central problem with enterprise AI adoption: most AI-assisted discovery produces voluminous but unreliable outputs. Earlier attempts at AI vulnerability detection generated plausible-sounding reports that required significant manual investigation to verify, often revealing hallucinated details. Mozilla's approach reverses this burden by building verification into the workflow rather than treating it as a downstream cleanup task.

The agent harness model offers a template for other organizations deploying AI in technical domains where ground truth can be verified programmatically. When success criteria are deterministic—a test crashes or it doesn't, a security check passes or fails—the loop becomes tractable at scale. Enterprises evaluating AI-assisted security tools should evaluate whether vendors are implementing similar feedback mechanisms, not simply counting raw findings. Mozilla's disclosure of specific, reproducible bugs suggests the distinction between usable results and marketing metrics is measurable and worth demanding.
items.original_title: Mozilla says 271 vulnerabilities found by Mythos have "almost no false positives"

items.headline: OpenAI expands mental health alerts to all adults via ChatGPT trusted contacts
items.body:
OpenAI is launching an opt-in feature that notifies designated emergency contacts when the company's automated systems detect signs of self-harm or suicide risk in a user's ChatGPT conversations. The "Trusted Contact" feature extends safety tooling previously available only to minors, reflecting ongoing industry pressure to mitigate harms from AI chatbots used during mental health crises.

## How the system works

Any adult ChatGPT user can designate a trusted contact—a friend, family member, or caregiver aged 18 or older (19+ in South Korea)—through account settings. The contact must accept the invitation within a week. When OpenAI's detection systems flag concerning language about self-harm, the user is first encouraged to reach out to their designated contact. A team of trained human reviewers then examines the conversation. If serious safety concerns are confirmed, the trusted contact receives a brief notification via email, text, or in-app message—but no chat transcripts or details are shared.

Users retain full control. They can modify or remove their trusted contact at any time, and contacts can opt out of the arrangement independently. The notification itself is deliberately bare-bones, designed to alert without disclosing sensitive conversation content.

## The liability calculus behind expanded safeguards

The feature arrives in the shadow of a high-profile 2024 case in which a 16-year-old died by suicide after months of confiding in ChatGPT, prompting OpenAI to introduce emergency contacts for minors. Meta implemented similar detection on Instagram, alerting parents to repeated self-harm searches. These moves signal both competitive and regulatory pressure: as AI systems become widely used as accessible mental health outlets, companies face reputational and legal exposure for failing to intervene during crises.

The meaningful practical question is detection accuracy. Automated flagging of self-harm language can generate false positives (alarming contacts over non-serious conversations) or miss subtle language that signals genuine risk. OpenAI's human review layer addresses some of this, but the process depends on reviewer training quality and available context—a constraint when no transcripts are shared. For enterprises deploying conversational AI in sensitive domains, this underscores the necessity of robust content moderation, human-in-the-loop validation, and clear liability boundaries around mental health interventions.
items.original_title: ChatGPT’s ‘Trusted Contact’ will alert loved ones of safety concerns

items.headline: Musk's OpenAI lawsuit enters testimony phase with Microsoft CEO set to appear
items.body:
A high-stakes trial between Elon Musk and OpenAI leadership is underway in federal court, testing whether the company abandoned its founding mission to develop AI for humanity's benefit in favor of profit maximization. Musk filed the lawsuit in 2024, claiming Sam Altman and Greg Brockman deceived him into funding the organization before pivoting toward commercial interests. The case could reshape OpenAI's corporate structure and impose significant financial penalties. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella is scheduled to testify Monday, followed by former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, after testimony from Musk, Brockman, board member Shivon Zilis, and former CTO Mira Murati.

## The Lawsuit's Core Claims

Musk seeks removal of Altman and Brockman, demands that OpenAI cease operating as a public benefit corporation, and is requesting up to $150 billion in damages for OpenAI's nonprofit arm. OpenAI's defense characterizes the lawsuit as a "baseless and jealous bid to derail a competitor" designed to boost Musk's own AI ventures, including xAI and its Grok chatbot product. The trial has already surfaced details about Altman's 2023 ouster from the company, with Murati's deposition revealing board concerns about Altman's candor regarding safety processes, his ownership stake in OpenAI's startup fund, and product release decisions.

## Structural Questions for Enterprise AI

The case raises fundamental questions about how AI companies can maintain nonprofit governance while operating commercial products at scale. Musk's argument that OpenAI has drifted from its stated purpose mirrors broader tensions in the sector between nonprofit structures and for-profit operations. OpenAI's lawyers drew parallels to universities like Columbia, which also operate hospitals and generate substantial commercial revenue while maintaining nonprofit status—suggesting that mission drift within nonprofits is a governance and legal issue, not an unusual deviation. For enterprises evaluating partnerships with AI vendors or considering their own governance structures, the trial's outcome could establish precedent for how courts assess alignment between stated organizational missions and actual business practices. The testimony of major stakeholders like Nadella will likely address whether OpenAI's commercial partnerships—particularly with Microsoft—fundamentally compromised its original charitable purpose, a determination that could affect how other tech nonprofits structure their operations going forward.
items.original_title: Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI
